notes: searches: ~2; full defenses: 0, cheap audits: 4. Scope per orchestrator: 4 anachronistic claims (048, 088, 097, 115), Read-only cheap audit each. Searches spent only on concrete leads: 048 (provenance of 'from Rome' marble) and 097 (Deuteronomy dating dispute + verifier internal-consistency check). 088 and 115 track Josephus directly and were audited without a search.

GC-01-048.argument: Two-part steelman. (1) Over-literalization: the sentence lists attributes that are each independently defensible even though their fusion is not. 'Massive blocks... of almost fabulous size' matches the real Herodian ashlars (the Western/retaining-wall megaliths, some >10 m, verifiably enormous). 'White marble' matches Josephus's own description (Wars 5.5.6: the temple faced with white stone, 'like a mountain covered with snow'), the primary and 19th-century-standard authority White inherited. 'Forwarded from Rome... formed a part of its structure' is not a pure fabrication: modern excavation (opus sectile floor tiles, colored/white stone) confirms decorative stone WAS imported from Rome, Italy, Asia Minor, Tunisia and Egypt and formed part of the temple complex's fabric. So the verifier's rationale ('imported marble used ONLY decoratively, and not brought from Rome as structural blocks') slightly overstates the negative: Roman marble genuinely was part of the structure, just not as the load-bearing megaliths. (2) Why it still fails: the natural unified reading — that the fabulous-size structural blocks were themselves white marble shipped from Rome — is false. The massive structural ashlars were local meleke limestone quarried near Jerusalem, not Roman marble. The claim conflates the limestone megaliths with the imported decorative marble. This is exactly a period-typical image (Josephus's white-marble temple, repeated by 19th-c. popular histories), so 'anachronistic' is the correct and appropriately generous verdict; it should not be upgraded to supported (the 'massive blocks = white marble from Rome' fusion is wrong) nor hardened to contradicted (each component has real support).

new_sources.title: Archeologists Restore Flooring That Adorned the Second Temple of Jerusalem
new_sources.note: Temple Mount opus sectile floor tiles were imported from Rome, Italy, Asia Minor, Tunisia, Egypt — imported stone (incl. from Rome) did form part of the temple's fabric, though the structural megaliths were local limestone.

GC-01-088.argument: Strongest case has two prongs. (a) The sub-claim that the siege resumed 'at the time of the Passover' is independently accurate — Titus invested Jerusalem around Passover of spring AD 70 (Josephus; accepted by modern historians), so that element is 'supported', not anachronistic. (b) 'Millions of Jews were assembled within its walls' faithfully reports Josephus, who explicitly gives 2,700,000 present at that Passover (Wars 6.9.3, from 256,500 paschal victims). White is thus not exaggerating beyond her primary source; a secondary reading takes 'millions' as 19th-c. rhetorical idiom for 'a vast multitude', which would make it a non-quantitative flourish (unverifiable) rather than a demographic error. Why it still fails: the sentence functions as a population statement and tracks Josephus's literal 2.7M, which modern demography rejects as grossly inflated (Jerusalem plus festival pilgrims numbered at most a few hundred thousand). The figure is wrong by modern standards but matched Josephus and the historiography White used — the textbook definition of 'anachronistic'. Verdict stands.

GC-01-097.argument: The verifier's own chapter applies an inconsistent standard, and that inconsistency is the catch. For GC-01-035 the verifier PASSED 'A thousand years before, the psalmist...' as supported with '[common_knowledge]... conventional rounding consistent with traditional Davidic-era psalm dating', and for GC-01-004 it passed Davidic authorship of Psalm 48 as supported — even though critical scholarship dates many psalms post-exilic and disputes Davidic authorship exactly as the documentary hypothesis disputes Mosaic authorship. Yet here it singles out the parallel traditional dating of Deuteronomy 28 ('the warning prophecy given fourteen centuries before') as anachronistic. Both are traditional scriptural attributions contested by the same body of critical scholarship; consistency demands the same non-accusatory treatment. On the merits: 'fourteen centuries before [AD 70]' is the traditional Mosaic dating (~1400 BC), which was universal in White's era and is still actively defended by conservative scholarship; White is dating the prophetic UTTERANCE per the biblical narrative's own internal chronology (Moses on the plains of Moab), not asserting a literary-composition date. The composition date and authorship of Deuteronomy 'remain hotly contested' (documentary hypothesis / de Wette's 7th-c. Josianic dating vs. traditional Mosaic authorship), so labeling her figure flatly 'wrong by modern standards' over-reaches. Finally, the rhetorical function — a prophecy uttered many centuries before its AD 70 fulfilment — holds under ANY of the proposed datings (1400 BC or 621 BC). Downgrading to 'disputed': the dating is a live authorship debate and a traditional attribution, not a settled modern error.

new_sources.title: Composition of the Torah — Wikipedia
new_sources.note: 'The precise process by which the Torah was composed, the number of authors involved, and the date of each author remain hotly contested' — the dating is not settled.

new_sources.title: Documentary hypothesis — New World Encyclopedia
new_sources.note: Sets out the traditional Mosaic-authorship position alongside de Wette's 621 BC dating of Deuteronomy; documents the standing disagreement rather than a unanimous verdict.

GC-01-115.argument: 'More than a million of the people perished' is a faithful, even conservative, restatement of Josephus (Wars 6.9.3), who gives 1,100,000 dead in the siege — the primary source and the 19th-century standard. White's immediately preceding block quotation at paragraphs 41-43 is H.H. Milman's 'History of the Jews' (Appendix note 46), and Milman likewise transmits the Josephan casualty figures; so this number traces directly and demonstrably to the sources White actually used, not to invention. A charitable reading also notes 'more than a million' is the floor of a range her sources gave, not a precise fabricated statistic. Why it still fails: modern demographic scholarship rejects Josephus's 1.1M as physically impossible for a city of Jerusalem's size plus festival pilgrims; realistic estimates of total war/siege deaths are far lower. The figure is therefore wrong by modern standards but consistent with the historiography White relied on — 'anachronistic', which is the correct verdict. Stands.
